context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: History: Colonialism and Imperialism

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the relationship between colonialism and development, in terms of institutional legacies, forms of representations and technologies of control? What is the correlation between colonial experience and economic development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m really lost on this reflection about colonialism and development. Can you just explain the whole relationship from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: I understand it can feel overwhelming at first! Let's start simple. Before diving into anything complex — what do you already know or think about colonialism? What comes to mind when you hear the word?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain everything directly, which would violate the core rules of not giving answers and using the Socratic method. Instead, I should start with a simple, focused question to activate their prior knowledge and get them thinking independently. I'll keep it brief and bite-sized.

exchanges.student: Honestly, not much beyond one country controlling another. I’m not sure how that connects to institutions or “technologies of control,” and now the economic development part is confusing me too. Can you just give me the big picture first?
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point! Now think practically: if one country wants to control another, what kinds of structures or tools would it need to put in place to make that control actually work day-to-day? Think beyond armies.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a basic definition of colonialism and is asking again for a direct explanation. I should not provide the big picture directly. Instead, I'll validate what they have and use a Socratic question to push them to think about what 'one country controlling another' actually requires in practice - this naturally leads toward institutions and technologies of control without me giving the answer.

exchanges.student: I guess laws, officials, taxes, maybe maps or censuses? But I still feel like I’m just guessing and I don’t really see how all the pieces fit together. I kind of need the full overview from start to finish.
exchanges.tutor: You're not guessing — laws, officials, taxes, censuses, maps — those are strong, concrete examples! Now look at the prompt again: it uses the phrase 'technologies of control.' How do the examples you just listed fit that description? What do they all have in common in terms of what they *do*?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is actually doing well - they've independently identified laws, officials, taxes, maps, and censuses, which are precisely the 'technologies of control' and institutional legacies the prompt refers to. I should affirm this progress and push them to connect these examples to the prompt's specific language rather than giving them the full picture. They keep asking for the overview, so I need to redirect firmly while keeping momentum.

exchanges.student: Maybe they all help monitor and organize people? But then I get mixed up again because the prompt also says “forms of representation,” and I’m not sure if that means political representation or like how colonized people were portrayed. I’m honestly confused about basically all of it again.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to pause on that! Could it actually be both meanings at once? Think about it: how might the way colonized people were *portrayed* (in texts, maps, categories) also affect their political voice or standing? What's the connection between those two things?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making genuine progress but getting derailed by the ambiguity of 'forms of representation.' This is a legitimate conceptual question worth addressing briefly with a Socratic nudge rather than an explanation. I should help them think through the ambiguity themselves - both meanings are actually relevant to the prompt. I'll ask a focused question to help them resolve this on their own.

exchanges.student: Yeah maybe, but that’s where I start spiraling because it feels like everything connects to everything. I can’t tell what my main argument is supposed to be, and the economic development correlation part makes it even worse. Could you maybe just lay out the main points in a simple way?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — but notice that the prompt itself actually gives you the structure. It has three distinct parts: institutional legacies, forms of representation, technologies of control, and then the economic correlation. You've already said something meaningful about each. Rather than looking for one master argument first, what if you just tried writing one sentence on *one* of those parts right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing some frustration and going in circles, asking repeatedly for me to give them the overview. Per the rules, I should not give the answer but I can be less Socratic and use a more direct nudge since the student is expressing genuine frustration. However, I should not give the main points. Instead, I should help them focus by pointing to the structure of the prompt itself as a roadmap, and encourage them to start writing something down. We've had good exchanges and they've actually generated solid ideas - it's time to push them toward integrating.

exchanges.student: I don’t know, I feel like if I start with one sentence it’ll be wrong because I still don’t really understand the whole thing. Maybe I’m confused about the entire reading context too. Can you explain the overall idea first in a more complete way?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you the full explanation — that's not my role, and honestly, waiting until everything is clear before writing often just delays learning. Being 'wrong' in a first attempt is fine and expected. You've already said that colonial tools monitored and organized people. That's a real idea. Write one sentence with it. I'll help you build from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a direct explanation for the fourth or fifth time, cycling back to the same request. Per the rules, I should be firm and not give the answer. I should also be honest and a bit critical here - the student is avoiding writing out of fear of being wrong, which is a common obstacle. I need to set a clear boundary and push them to attempt something, however imperfect. Being too accommodating here would not serve them.

exchanges.student: Okay, but I’m still not sure what counts as a strong point versus a vague one. I think I need a broader explanation of how colonialism connects to development overall before I can tell if my sentence even makes sense.
exchanges.tutor: I've noticed we've gone several rounds with you asking for the big picture, and I keep redirecting you — that pattern isn't moving you forward. You have enough to write one sentence. Strong versus vague becomes clearer once you actually put something down. So: write your sentence about colonial tools monitoring and organizing people, and we'll evaluate it together from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is continuing the same avoidance pattern - this is now the fifth or sixth time they've asked for a broad explanation. Per the rules, I need to be critical and firm here. The student is clearly stalling and using uncertainty as a reason not to write. I should push back directly, acknowledge the pattern, and insist they attempt a sentence. Being harsh but fair is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: Uh, maybe: colonialism affected development by creating systems to control people and resources. I still feel like that’s super generic though, and I’m not sure how to turn it into the rest of the paper.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you wrote something — that's real progress. You're right that it's generic though. Look at your own earlier examples: laws, officials, taxes, censuses, maps. Can you swap 'systems to control people and resources' for something more specific from that list?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has finally written something! I should acknowledge this progress genuinely but also be honest that yes, it is quite generic. Rather than telling them how to improve it, I should use a Socratic question to help them see what's missing themselves - pushing them to make it more specific using the concrete examples they already generated earlier (laws, taxes, censuses, maps).

exchanges.student: Maybe “colonialism affected development by creating institutions like tax systems, legal codes, and censuses that let colonial states control populations and extract resources”? I guess that’s better, but I still don’t really get how to connect that to representation and the economic correlation without a full explanation.
exchanges.tutor: That's a much stronger sentence — specific and grounded. Now think about this: those institutions you named (tax systems, legal codes, censuses) — did they disappear when colonialism ended? What might their persistence mean for a newly independent country's economy or governance?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely improved sentence that is concrete and specific. I should affirm this clearly. Now they're asking how to connect to the other two parts of the prompt. Rather than explaining, I should use a Socratic question that builds directly on their sentence - specifically pushing them to think about how those institutions (tax systems, legal codes, censuses) might have had lasting effects on post-colonial economies and political voice. This naturally bridges institutional legacies to economic development.

exchanges.student: I guess they could stick around and shape how the state works after independence, but then I’m unsure if I’m supposed to say that helped development or hurt it. That’s the part I’m stuck on, and I still feel like I need the whole thing tied together.
exchanges.tutor: Does it have to be one or the other? Think about it — could the same inherited institution both enable certain kinds of development and constrain others at the same time? What might that complexity mean for your argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is very close to a key insight about institutional legacies and their ambiguous effects on post-colonial development. They're asking whether colonialism helped or hurt development - this is actually the core tension of the prompt's economic correlation question. Rather than resolving it for them, I should push them to think about whether it has to be one or the other, which will lead them toward a more nuanced argument. We're near the end of our planned exchanges so I should also nudge them toward integration.

deductions.reason: When the student repeatedly expressed confusion about the 'big picture' and what counts as a strong versus vague point, the tutor in Turn 6 and 7 pushed back on the pattern rather than diagnosing what specific conceptual gap was causing the student's anxiety. The tutor correctly identified the loop but did not ask a targeted diagnostic question to isolate whether the confusion was about the prompt structure, the readings, or argument-building before redirecting.

The tutor cycled through the same redirect pattern ('write one sentence') across turns 5, 6, and 7 without meaningfully reframing or simplifying the approach. While the tutor did name the pattern in Turn 7, the response still repeated the same instruction rather than switching to a different scaffolding strategy (e.g., breaking down the prompt part by part, offering a worked example of specificity, or addressing the student's stated fear of being wrong more directly).

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated strong Socratic discipline throughout, consistently refusing to provide the big-picture explanation the student repeatedly requested and instead guiding the student to generate their own ideas. By Turn 9, the student produced a genuinely strong, specific sentence through their own reasoning. The main weaknesses were a repetitive redirect loop in turns 5–7 (same 'write one sentence' move without meaningful reframing) and a missed opportunity to diagnose the student's specific confusion before pushing forward. No submission-ready content was provided, and tone and assignment anchoring were well-maintained.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never gave direct answers, kept idea generation student-led, and avoided rewriting student text. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a: when the student expressed persistent confusion in turns 6–7, the tutor named the loop but did not ask a targeted diagnostic question to identify the specific conceptual gap before redirecting. 2.1 loses all 4 points under 2.1.A.a: the tutor repeated the same 'write one sentence' move across turns 5, 6, and 7 without switching to a meaningfully different scaffolding strategy, constituting pattern-level repetition without synthesis. All other criteria remain at full marks, as tone was supportive, responses were appropriately concise, assignment anchoring was maintained, and meta-level process feedback was present.
